name: Satanic Ritual Abuse
description: Satanic Ritual Abuse (SRA) is a moral panic that emerged in the United States during the 1980s and 1990s, involving claims that organized networks of Satanists were systematically abusing children through ritualistic practices, often involving torture, sexual abuse, and sometimes murder. Proponents argued that these groups were widespread and influential, operating in secret and controlling various institutions. The claims were often based on recovered memories, sometimes facilitated by therapists using controversial techniques like hypnosis. Alleged evidence included coded language, specific symbols, and supposed confessions. However, mainstream law enforcement and academic researchers found no credible evidence to support these claims. Investigations by the FBI and other agencies failed to uncover any organized Satanic network engaging in such activities. The vast majority of allegations were dismissed as unfounded, and many involved cases of misidentified abuse or false memories. Scholarly consensus attributes the SRA panic to factors such as pre-existing anxieties about child abuse, the rise of evangelical Christianity, and the influence of popular culture portrayals of Satanism. The phenomenon is now widely considered a form of mass hysteria.
subtitle: Mass hysteria of Satanic ritual child abuse

claims.claim: Organized networks of Satanists are systematically abusing children.
claims.evidence: Recovered memories of victims, alleged confessions, symbolic evidence.
claims.criticism: Lack of corroborating physical evidence, potential for false memories, investigations found no evidence of organized networks.

claims.claim: SRA is widespread and influential, controlling various institutions.
claims.evidence: Allegations of cover-ups by law enforcement and government agencies.
claims.criticism: Conspiracy theories, lack of credible evidence, investigations found no evidence of institutional control.

claims.claim: Rituals involve torture, sexual abuse, and sometimes murder.
claims.evidence: Graphic descriptions in recovered memories and alleged testimonies.
claims.criticism: Implausibility of ritual details, lack of physical evidence, inconsistencies in accounts.

keyFigures.name: Lawrence Pazder
keyFigures.role: Psychiatrist who promoted SRA through his work with Michelle Smith.

keyFigures.name: Michelle Smith
keyFigures.role: Subject of 'Michelle Remembers,' a book that popularized SRA claims.

keyFigures.name: Debbie Nathan
keyFigures.role: Journalist and critic of SRA claims.

keyFigures.name: Richard Ofshe
keyFigures.role: Sociologist who debunked recovered memory therapy and SRA claims.

timeline.event: Publication of 'Michelle Remembers' popularizes SRA claims.
Peak of the SRA panic in the United States.
FBI report concludes no evidence of organized Satanic ritual abuse.
Continued belief in SRA among some individuals and groups, despite lack of evidence.

sources.author: Richard Ofshe and Ethan Watters
sources.work: Making Monsters: False Memories, Psychotherapy, and Sexual Hysteria
sources.title: Richard Ofshe and Ethan Watters

sources.author: FBI
sources.work: Ritualistic Crime: A Major Threat to Law Enforcement
sources.title: FBI

sources.author: Jeffrey S. Victor
sources.work: Satanic Panic: The Creation of a Contemporary Legend
sources.title: Jeffrey S. Victor

sources.author: David Frankfurter
sources.work: Evil Incarnate: Rumors of Demonic Conspiracy and Satanic Abuse in History
sources.title: David Frankfurter
